original_abstract: Achieving economic growth through the lens of sustainable development has become an essential mandate for both developed and developing nations. The traditional industrialization process, primarily fueled by the unconscious consumption of natural resources and heavy reliance on fossil fuels, has inflicted unprecedented damage on the global ecosystem. Furthermore, the wealth generated by this historical transformation has failed to bridge social inequalities or create an employment landscape sufficient to meet the rising global labor supply. This study examines the emergence of "green jobs" since the late 1980s as a dual solution for promoting ecological sustainability and absorbing the expanding workforce. By analyzing the structural shifts in the labor market, the research highlights how transitioning to a green economy can foster decent work, enhance social welfare, and ensure long-term economic resilience in harmony with nature.
humanized_abstract: Achieving economic growth in line with sustainable development has become a priority for virtually all countries, developed and developing. The economic growth that accompanied industrialisation over the past two centuries was achieved at the cost of huge environmental degradation due to the unplanned character of resource use and the dependence on fossil fuels. The benefits of this growth have not been evenly spread and the resulting employment has not kept pace with the growing labour force. The study analyses the development of so-called green jobs since the late 1980s as a means of addressing both the environmental challenge and the labour force challenge. The study examines the changes in the labour market and how an economy can be restructured to achieve environmentally-friendly growth and decent work and improve the living and working conditions of people and thus ensure sustainable employment and greater economic stability.
